text: The surgeon encounters adhesions of the omentum to the top of the gallbladder, which is tensely distended and difficult to grasp due to adherent pericolic fat and more omentum. The surgeon carefully dissects these adhesions to identify the gallbladder wall. The duodenum is drawn up and needs to be taken down to visualize the bottom of the gallbladder.

The surgeon attempts to clear the peritoneum around the gallbladder, which is chronically thickened and inflamed, making dissection challenging. Lateral mobilization of the gallbladder is performed to aid in retraction and exposure. An inadvertent hole is made in the gallbladder, revealing pus inside. The surgeon continues to work towards identifying the bottom of the gallbladder and its junction with the cystic duct amidst unforgiving tissues.

The surgeon identifies the borders of the Calot's triangle and clears the backside of the anticipated duct location, facing difficulty due to angles and gallbladder positioning. The artery is identified and freed up, facilitating less bloody dissection. The surgeon examines and removes peritoneum from the inferior surface of the duct, gaining direct access to the duct.

The surgeon clips and transects the cystic duct after confirming proper anatomy with a cholangiogram. The posterior cystic artery is identified and addressed. The gallbladder is then carefully dissected off the liver bed. The surgical field is cleaned, ensuring hemostasis, with no visible bleeding. The area appears dry, and no drain is left in place.
